summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is stratified sampling?

Stratified sampling divides the population into distinct subgroups (strata) based on a shared characteristic, then takes a random sample from each stratum.

What is cluster sampling?

In cluster sampling, the population is divided into groups (clusters), some clusters are randomly selected, and all individuals within chosen clusters are studied.

What is the difference between random sampling and random assignment?

Random sampling determines which individuals are chosen from the population for the study, while random assignment determines which treatment each participant receives.

What is 'multistage sampling'?

Multistage sampling uses multiple sampling methods in sequence, such as first selecting clusters, then sampling individuals within those clusters.

A television station posts an online poll asking viewers, 'Do you support the new policy?' Of the 10,000 viewers who see the poll, only 800 respond, with 85% opposing the policy. What is the primary concern?

Voluntary response samples attract people with strong opinions, likely over-representing extreme viewpoints and skewing results.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: Under-coverage occurs when:

Under-coverage means certain groups are left out or under-represented in the sampling frame.

Example: What is 'multistage sampling'?

Multistage sampling uses multiple sampling methods in sequence, such as first selecting clusters, then sampling individuals within those clusters.

Example: A researcher numbers all 10,000 employees at a company and uses a random number generator to select 500. This is an example of:

Using a random number generator to select individuals directly from a numbered list is simple random sampling.

Example: A company has 5,000 employees divided into 3 departments: Sales (2,000), Engineering (1,500), and Support (1,500). To ensure each department is represented, the HR team randomly selects 40 employees from each department. However, a colleague argues this gives Support employees a higher chance of selection than Sales employees. Is this a valid concern?

In proportional stratified sampling, the number selected from each stratum should be proportional to the stratum's share of the population, ensuring equal selection probabilities.
